schools.summary: The calling of Aaron and his sons to the priesthood: 'and take thou unto thee Aaron thy brother, and his sons with him, from among the children of Israel, that he may minister unto me in the priest's office, even Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar, Aaron's sons' — God calling Aaron and his four sons to the priesthood, set apart from among the people to minister to God (the calling, the priesthood by divine appointment, not self-chosen, Hebrews 5:4, 'no man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron,' the office, to minister to God, the mediation between God and the people); the priest's office (the ministering to God, the offering of sacrifice and incense, the mediation) — the figure (the Aaronic priesthood a type of Christ, the great High Priest, Hebrews 4:14-15; 5:1-10, the priest called of God, ministering, mediating); the four sons of Aaron named (Nadab and Abihu, who would die offering strange fire, Leviticus 10:1-2; Eleazar and Ithamar, who would continue the line, the providence, the priesthood established in Aaron's house, the type of Christ's priesthood) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And take thou unto thee Aaron thy brother, and his sons with him... that he may minister unto me in the priest's office
the calling of Aaron and his sons to the priesthood
the priesthood by divine appointment, not self-chosen (Hebrews 5:4)
a type of Christ the great High Priest (Hebrews 4:14-15; 5:1-10)

schools.summary: The holy garments for glory and beauty: 'and thou shalt make holy garments for Aaron thy brother for glory and for beauty' — special holy garments to be made for Aaron the high priest (the holy garments, set apart, consecrated, distinct from common dress, the vestments of the high priest, the ephod, breastplate, robe, mitre, etc., vv.4, the consecration in the garments); the design — 'for glory and for beauty': the garments for glory and beauty (splendor, dignity, honor, the witnesses noting the meaning — the high priest's garments magnificent, adorned with gold, precious stones, and rich colors, marking the dignity and glory of the office, the lesson — the glory and beauty a figure of the glory of the priesthood, and ultimately of Christ the great High Priest, the garments of glory and beauty a figure of Christ's glory and his perfect priestly adornment, the beauty of his person and office, Psalm 45:2, the high priest's splendor pointing to Christ, 'crowned with glory and honour,' Hebrews 2:9, the garments of salvation and the robe of righteousness, Isaiah 61:10, the dignity of the office God himself adorned); the holy garments for glory and beauty — the dignity of the priesthood (God clothing his priest in splendor, the providence, the garments a figure of the glory of Christ, and of the righteousness in which the saints are clothed, Revelation 19:8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What do the garments 'for glory and for beauty' signify?

sides.position: The magnificent vestments marking the dignity and glory of the priesthood, a figure of the glory of Christ the great High Priest (Hebrews 2:9; Psalm 45:2) and of the robe of righteousness in which the saints are clothed (Isaiah 61:10; Revelation 19:8) - the office God himself adorned with glory

themes: And thou shalt make holy garments for Aaron thy brother for glory and for beauty
the holy garments for glory and beauty (the crux of the priestly glory)
the magnificent vestments marking the dignity and glory of the priesthood
a figure of the glory of Christ the High Priest and the robe of righteousness (Hebrews 2:9; Isaiah 61:10)

schools.summary: The wise-hearted workmen filled with the Spirit: 'and thou shalt speak unto all that are wise hearted, whom I have filled with the spirit of wisdom, that they may make Aaron's garments to consecrate him, that he may minister unto me in the priest's office' — God appointing wise-hearted workmen (skilled craftsmen), whom he had filled with the spirit of wisdom, to make the garments (the wise-hearted, the skilled artificers, Bezaleel and Aholiab and others, Exodus 31:1-6; 35:30-35, the spirit of wisdom, the skill itself a gift of God's Spirit, the craftsmanship divinely given); the spirit of wisdom (the skill, the craftsmanship, the artistic ability, all a gift of God's Spirit) — the lesson (all skill and ability God's gift, the Spirit the source even of the arts and crafts, James 1:17, 'every good gift... cometh down from the Father,' the work of God's house done by Spirit-given skill); 'that they may make Aaron's garments to consecrate him, that he may minister unto me in the priest's office' (the garments for the consecration, the investing of the priest, the setting apart by the garments, the providence, the Spirit-filled workmen making the garments for the consecration of the priest, the work of God's house by God's gifts) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And thou shalt speak unto all that are wise hearted, whom I have filled with the spirit of wisdom, that they may make Aaron's garments to consecrate him
the wise-hearted workmen filled with the Spirit
the skill and craftsmanship a gift of God's Spirit (Exodus 31:1-6; James 1:17)
the work of God's house done by Spirit-given skill

schools.summary: The list of the holy garments: 'and these are the garments which they shall make; a breastplate, and an ephod, and a robe, and a broidered coat, a mitre, and a girdle: and they shall make holy garments for Aaron thy brother, and his sons, that he may minister unto me in the priest's office' — the high priest's garments listed: the breastplate (vv.15-30), the ephod (vv.6-14), the robe (vv.31-35), the broidered (woven) coat, the mitre (turban, vv.36-38), and the girdle (sash, the six garments of the high priest, the full vestments of glory and beauty, each with its meaning); the breastplate, ephod, and robe (the chief and most significant garments, the breastplate over the heart, the ephod on the shoulders, the robe of blue) — the figures (the high priest's garments figures of Christ's priestly glory and his bearing of his people); the broidered coat (the woven tunic), the mitre (the turban, with the golden plate, vv.36), and the girdle (the sash) — the full vestments (the providence, the six garments of the high priest, the figures of Christ's perfect priesthood and adornment) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And these are the garments which they shall make; a breastplate, and an ephod, and a robe, and a broidered coat, a mitre, and a girdle
the list of the holy garments
the six garments of the high priest (vv.6-38)
the figures of Christ's perfect priestly glory and adornment

schools.summary: The materials of the priestly garments: 'and they shall take gold, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine linen' — the materials for the garments: gold (threads of gold), blue, purple, scarlet (the rich dyes), and fine linen (white, the materials, the same rich materials as the tabernacle curtains and veil, the gold and the colors and the linen, the figures, the gold of glory, blue of heaven, purple of royalty, scarlet of sacrifice, white linen of purity); the gold and the colors (woven into the ephod and breastplate) — the rich materials (the figures, the gold of deity/glory, blue of heaven, purple of kingship, scarlet of blood/sacrifice); 'and fine linen' (the fine white linen, the figure of purity and righteousness, Revelation 19:8, the materials of glory and beauty, the providence, the rich materials, the figures of Christ's heavenly, kingly, sacrificial, and pure glory, woven into the high priest's garments) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And they shall take gold, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine linen
the materials of the priestly garments
the rich materials - gold, blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen
the figures of Christ's heavenly, kingly, sacrificial, and pure glory (Revelation 19:8)

schools.summary: The making of the ephod: 'and they shall make the ephod of gold, of blue, and of purple, of scarlet, and fine twined linen, with cunning work' — the ephod (the chief garment of the high priest, a sort of vest or apron worn over the robe, of gold and the rich colors and fine linen, of cunning, skilled, artistic, work, the ephod, the principal vestment, bearing the shoulder-stones and the breastplate, vv.7-12, the rich materials, gold and the colors, the glory of the garment); the ephod (the chief garment, of gold and the colors) — the figure (the ephod a figure of Christ's priestly glory, or of the church the high priest bears); 'with cunning work' (the cunning, artistic, skilled, with woven figures, work, the glory and beauty of the garment, the providence, the ephod the chief garment, bearing the names of the tribes on the shoulders and the breastplate on the heart, the figure of the high priest bearing his people, the type of Christ) — Henry, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And they shall make the ephod of gold, of blue, and of purple, of scarlet, and fine twined linen, with cunning work
the making of the ephod
the chief garment of the high priest, bearing the shoulder-stones and breastplate
the figure of the high priest bearing his people, the type of Christ

schools.summary: The two shoulderpieces of the ephod: 'it shall have the two shoulderpieces thereof joined at the two edges thereof; and so it shall be joined together' — the ephod having two shoulderpieces (the parts going over the shoulders), joined at the two edges (front and back joined at the shoulders, the shoulderpieces, the parts that bore the onyx stones with the tribes' names, vv.9-12, the joining, the front and back of the ephod united at the shoulders, the structure of the garment); the two shoulderpieces (over the shoulders, bearing the stones, vv.12) — the structure (the figure, the shoulders, the place of strength, bearing the burden of the people); 'so it shall be joined together' (the front and back joined at the shoulders, the ephod made one garment, the providence, the shoulderpieces bearing the names of the tribes, the high priest bearing the people on his shoulders of strength, vv.12, the figure of Christ) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Gill.

themes: It shall have the two shoulderpieces thereof joined at the two edges thereof; and so it shall be joined together
the two shoulderpieces of the ephod
the shoulders that would bear the names of the tribes (vv.12)
the high priest bearing the people on his shoulders of strength

schools.summary: The curious girdle of the ephod: 'and the curious girdle of the ephod, which is upon it, shall be of the same, according to the work thereof; even of gold, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen' — the 'curious girdle' (the skillfully woven band, the belt of the ephod) of the same materials and work as the ephod (gold and the colors and fine linen, the girdle, the band that bound the ephod to the body, the figure, the girdle, the binding, perhaps a figure of the readiness for service, the girded priest, or of Christ girded for his priestly work, Isaiah 11:5); the curious girdle (the woven band of the ephod, of the same materials) — the binding (the part of the garment); 'of gold, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen' (the same rich materials as the ephod, the workmanship, the providence, the girdle binding the ephod, the girded priest ready for service, the figure of Christ girded for his work) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the curious girdle of the ephod, which is upon it, shall be of the same... even of gold, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen
the curious girdle of the ephod
the woven band binding the ephod, of the same rich materials
the figure of the priest girded for service, of Christ girded for his work (Isaiah 11:5)

schools.summary: The two onyx stones engraved with the tribes' names: 'and thou shalt take two onyx stones, and grave on them the names of the children of Israel' — two onyx stones, engraved with the names of the twelve tribes (six on each, vv.10), set on the shoulders of the ephod (the stones, the precious onyx, for the shoulder-pieces, vv.12, the names engraved, the twelve tribes, the people, borne on the high priest's shoulders); the two onyx stones (precious, set on the shoulders) — the bearing of the names (the tribes engraved, borne by the high priest); 'grave on them the names of the children of Israel' (the names of the tribes engraved, the people borne on the high priest's shoulders, vv.12, the figure, the high priest bearing the names of the people before God, a figure of Christ bearing his people, the names graven and borne, the providence, the tribes engraved on the stones, the people carried by the priest) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And thou shalt take two onyx stones, and grave on them the names of the children of Israel
the two onyx stones engraved with the tribes' names
the tribes engraved and borne on the high priest's shoulders (vv.12)
a figure of Christ bearing his people

schools.summary: The tribes divided on the two stones: 'six of their names on one stone, and the other six names of the rest on the other stone, according to their birth' — six tribes' names on one onyx stone, six on the other, in the order of their birth (the order of the sons of Jacob, the division, the twelve tribes divided between the two shoulder-stones, the order, according to their birth, the natural order of the sons, the figure, all twelve tribes, the whole people, borne on the shoulders); the six and six (the twelve divided between the two stones) — the equal bearing (the whole people); 'according to their birth' (the names in the order of birth, the sons of Jacob in their natural order, the providence, the whole twelve tribes borne, in order, on the high priest's shoulders, the figure of Christ bearing all his people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Six of their names on one stone, and the other six names of the rest on the other stone, according to their birth
the tribes divided on the two stones
all twelve tribes borne in order on the high priest's shoulders
a figure of Christ bearing all his people

schools.summary: The engraving and setting of the stones: 'with the work of an engraver in stone, like the engravings of a signet, shalt thou engrave the two stones with the names of the children of Israel: thou shalt make them to be set in ouches of gold' — the names engraved on the stones with the skill of a gem-engraver (like a signet, deeply and clearly cut), and the stones set in ouches (settings, sockets) of gold (the engraving, the names cut as a signet, permanent, clear, the setting, the stones in gold settings, on the shoulders); the engraving like a signet (deeply, permanently cut, as a seal) — the permanence (the names graven, not to be effaced, the figure, the people graven, as on the palms of God's hands, Isaiah 49:16, 'I have graven thee upon the palms of my hands'); 'set in ouches of gold' (the stones in gold settings, the precious stones held in gold, the glory of the bearing, the providence, the names graven and set in gold on the shoulders, the people borne in glory, the figure of Christ bearing his people graven and precious) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: With the work of an engraver in stone, like the engravings of a signet, shalt thou engrave the two stones with the names of the children of Israel: thou shalt make them to be set in ouches of gold
the engraving and setting of the stones
the names permanently graven like a signet, set in gold
a figure of Christ bearing his people graven and precious (Isaiah 49:16)

schools.summary: The high priest bearing the tribes on his shoulders: 'and thou shalt put the two stones upon the shoulders of the ephod for stones of memorial unto the children of Israel: and Aaron shall bear their names before the LORD upon his two shoulders for a memorial' — the two onyx stones (with the tribes' names) set on the shoulders of the ephod as 'stones of memorial,' that Aaron might bear the names of the tribes before the LORD on his shoulders (the memorial, the names borne before God, the people remembered, presented before the LORD by the high priest); the figure — the high priest bearing the names of the tribes on his shoulders (the place of strength, the burden borne, the witnesses noting the lesson — the high priest bearing the people on his shoulders, the place of strength and burden-bearing, presenting them before God, a figure of Christ the great High Priest bearing his people, the shoulders the place of strength, the burden of the people borne by the priest's strength, as the shepherd bears the sheep on his shoulders, Luke 15:5, Christ bearing his people on his strength, upholding them, carrying them, Isaiah 40:11; 46:4; Deuteronomy 33:12, the people presented before God, borne and remembered by the priest); 'for a memorial' (the names borne for a memorial, the people remembered before God, presented continually, the providence, the high priest bearing the people on his shoulders before God, the figure of Christ bearing and presenting his people, upholding them by his strength) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

debates.question: What does the high priest bearing the names on his shoulders signify?

sides.position: The high priest bearing the people on his shoulders, the place of strength and burden-bearing, presenting them before God - a figure of Christ bearing his people on his strength (Isaiah 40:11; 46:4; Luke 15:5), upholding and carrying them, the people remembered and presented before God

themes: And thou shalt put the two stones upon the shoulders of the ephod for stones of memorial unto the children of Israel: and Aaron shall bear their names before the LORD upon his two shoulders for a memorial
the high priest bearing the tribes on his shoulders (the crux of bearing the people on his strength)
the people borne on the shoulders, the place of strength, presented before God
a figure of Christ bearing his people on his strength (Isaiah 40:11; 46:4; Luke 15:5)

schools.summary: The gold settings for the stones: 'and thou shalt make ouches of gold' — the ouches (settings, sockets, filigree-work of gold) made to hold the onyx stones (vv.11) and to receive the chains that joined the breastplate to the ephod (vv.14, 25, the ouches, the gold settings, the precious work, the provision, the gold settings for the stones and the chains, joining the breastplate to the shoulder-pieces); the ouches of gold (the settings for the onyx stones, and for the chains, vv.14) — the gold work (the joining of the breastplate to the ephod); the ouches of gold (the settings, the filigree, the precious gold work) — the provision (the gold settings holding the stones and the chains, the breastplate joined to the shoulders, the people on the shoulders and on the heart joined, the providence, the gold work of the garment, the glory and the joining) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make ouches of gold
the gold settings for the stones
the settings holding the stones and the chains (vv.14)
joining the breastplate to the shoulders

schools.summary: The chains of wreathen gold: 'and two chains of pure gold at the ends; of wreathen work shalt thou make them, and fasten the wreathen chains to the ouches' — two chains of pure gold, of wreathen (twisted, braided) work, fastened to the ouches (settings), to join the breastplate to the shoulder-pieces of the ephod (vv.22-25, the chains, the gold cords joining the breastplate to the ephod, the wreathen work, twisted, like cords or ropes of gold, the joining, the breastplate on the heart joined to the stones on the shoulders); the wreathen gold chains (twisted cords of gold, joining the breastplate to the ephod) — the joining (the gold cords); 'fasten the wreathen chains to the ouches' (the chains fastened to the gold settings, joining the breastplate to the shoulders, vv.25, the providence, the breastplate on the heart joined by gold chains to the stones on the shoulders, the people on the heart and the shoulders joined, the figure of the priest bearing the people in love and strength) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And two chains of pure gold at the ends; of wreathen work shalt thou make them, and fasten the wreathen chains to the ouches
the chains of wreathen gold
the gold cords joining the breastplate to the shoulder-pieces (vv.22-25)
the people borne on heart and shoulders joined, in love and strength

schools.summary: The making of the breastplate of judgment: 'and thou shalt make the breastplate of judgment with cunning work; after the work of the ephod thou shalt make it; of gold, of blue, and of purple, and of scarlet, and of fine twined linen, shalt thou make it' — the breastplate of judgment (a square pouch, of the same rich materials as the ephod, cunning work) worn on the high priest's breast (the breastplate, the chief ornament, bearing the twelve stones, vv.17-21, and the Urim and Thummim, vv.30, the 'breastplate of judgment,' named for the judgment it bore, the Urim and Thummim by which God's judgment was sought, vv.30); the breastplate of judgment (the square ornament on the breast, of the same materials as the ephod) — the chief ornament (the figure, the breastplate bearing the tribes on the heart, the Urim and Thummim, the judgment); 'of gold, of blue, and of purple, and of scarlet, and of fine twined linen' (the same rich materials as the ephod, gold and the colors and fine linen, the workmanship, the providence, the breastplate of judgment, bearing the tribes on the heart and the Urim and Thummim, the figure of the high priest bearing the people in love and seeking God's judgment for them, the type of Christ) — Henry, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And thou shalt make the breastplate of judgment with cunning work... of gold, of blue, and of purple, and of scarlet, and of fine twined linen
the making of the breastplate of judgment
the chief ornament bearing the twelve stones and the Urim and Thummim (vv.17-30)
the figure of the high priest bearing the people and seeking God's judgment, the type of Christ

schools.summary: The breastplate foursquare and doubled: 'foursquare it shall be being doubled; a span shall be the length thereof, and a span shall be the breadth thereof' — the breastplate foursquare (a perfect square, a span each way, about nine inches), doubled (folded double, to make a pouch, perhaps to hold the Urim and Thummim, vv.30, the shape, the square, the perfect symmetry, the doubling, folded, making a pocket, the dimensions, a span square); the breastplate foursquare and doubled (the square pouch, folded double) — the shape (the pocket, for the Urim and Thummim); 'a span shall be the length thereof, and a span shall be the breadth thereof' (the span square, about nine inches each way, the perfect square, the symmetry, perhaps the figure of perfection, the providence, the breastplate foursquare, bearing the twelve tribes in perfect order, the figure of the perfect bearing of the people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Foursquare it shall be being doubled; a span shall be the length thereof, and a span shall be the breadth thereof
the breastplate foursquare and doubled
the square pouch, folded double to hold the Urim and Thummim (vv.30)
bearing the twelve tribes in perfect order

schools.summary: The twelve precious stones set in the breastplate: 'and thou shalt set in it settings of stones, even four rows of stones: the first row shall be a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle: this shall be the first row' — twelve precious stones, in four rows of three, set in the breastplate, each engraved with a tribe's name (vv.21, the first row, sardius, topaz, carbuncle, the stones, the twelve tribes, each a precious stone, borne on the heart of the high priest); the four rows of three (the twelve stones, the twelve tribes) — the order (the figure, each tribe a precious stone, distinct, valued, borne on the heart); 'the first row shall be a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle' (the first three stones, each precious, distinct, the figure, the tribes as precious stones, the people God's jewels, Malachi 3:17, the precious stones of the church, the New Jerusalem garnished with all manner of precious stones, Revelation 21:19-20, the providence, the tribes as precious stones borne on the high priest's heart) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And thou shalt set in it settings of stones, even four rows of stones: the first row shall be a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle
the twelve precious stones set in the breastplate
the twelve tribes as precious stones, borne on the high priest's heart
God's jewels (Malachi 3:17), the precious stones of the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:19-20)

schools.summary: The second row of stones: 'and the second row shall be an emerald, a sapphire, and a diamond' — the second of the four rows of the breastplate holding an emerald, a sapphire, and a diamond, each a precious stone engraved with a tribe's name (the second row, three more of the twelve stones, the stones, each precious, distinct, a tribe borne on the heart); the emerald and sapphire (precious stones, the second row) — the jewels (the tribes); 'and a diamond' (the diamond, a precious stone, completing the second row, the figure, the tribes as varied, distinct, precious stones, each different, all valued, borne on the heart, the providence, the twelve stones the twelve tribes, the people God's jewels, Revelation 21:19-20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the second row shall be an emerald, a sapphire, and a diamond
the second row of stones
three more of the twelve tribes as precious stones
the people God's jewels (Revelation 21:19-20)

schools.summary: The third row of stones: 'and the third row a ligure, an agate, and an amethyst' — the third row of the breastplate holding a ligure (jacinth), an agate, and an amethyst, each a precious stone engraved with a tribe's name (the third row, three more of the twelve stones, the stones, each precious, distinct, a tribe borne on the heart); the ligure and agate (precious stones, the third row) — the jewels (the tribes); 'and an amethyst' (the amethyst, a precious stone, completing the third row, the figure, the tribes as varied, precious stones, each distinct, all borne on the heart, the providence, the twelve stones the twelve tribes, the people God's jewels, the precious stones of the church, Revelation 21:19-20) — Gill.

themes: And the third row a ligure, an agate, and an amethyst
the third row of stones
three more of the twelve tribes as precious stones
the precious stones of the church (Revelation 21:19-20)

schools.summary: The fourth row of stones set in gold: 'and the fourth row a beryl, and an onyx, and a jasper: they shall be set in gold in their inclosings' — the fourth row holding a beryl, an onyx, and a jasper, completing the twelve stones, all the stones set in gold (in their settings, inclosings, the twelve stones complete, the four rows of three, the twelve tribes, the gold settings, the precious stones held in gold); the beryl, onyx, and jasper (the fourth row, completing the twelve) — the jewels (the tribes); 'they shall be set in gold in their inclosings' (all the stones set in gold, the precious stones held in gold settings, the glory, the twelve tribes as precious stones in gold, borne on the heart, the providence, the twelve stones the twelve tribes, set in gold, the people God's jewels, precious and held secure, Malachi 3:17) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the fourth row a beryl, and an onyx, and a jasper: they shall be set in gold in their inclosings
the fourth row of stones set in gold
the twelve tribes complete as precious stones in gold
the people God's jewels, precious and held secure (Malachi 3:17)

schools.summary: The twelve stones engraved with the tribes' names: 'and the stones shall be with the names of the children of Israel, twelve, according to their names, like the engravings of a signet; every one with his name shall they be according to the twelve tribes' — the twelve stones each bearing a tribe's name, engraved like a signet (deeply, permanently, clearly, the names, each tribe distinct, named, on its own precious stone, the engraving like a signet, permanent, as the shoulder-stones, vv.11); the twelve names engraved like a signet (each tribe on its stone, permanent) — the figure (each tribe distinct, named, valued, graven, borne on the heart, the people graven, as on the palms of God's hands, Isaiah 49:16); 'every one with his name shall they be according to the twelve tribes' (each tribe named, distinct, in order, the figure, the whole people, each tribe distinct and named, borne on the high priest's heart, a figure of Christ knowing and bearing each of his people by name, John 10:3, 'he calleth his own sheep by name,' the providence, the tribes graven and borne, each named, on the heart) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the stones shall be with the names of the children of Israel, twelve, according to their names, like the engravings of a signet; every one with his name shall they be according to the twelve tribes
the twelve stones engraved with the tribes' names
each tribe distinct, named, graven like a signet, borne on the heart (Isaiah 49:16)
a figure of Christ knowing and bearing each of his people by name (John 10:3)

schools.summary: The wreathen chains of the breastplate: 'and thou shalt make upon the breastplate chains at the ends of wreathen work of pure gold' — wreathen (twisted, braided) chains of pure gold made at the ends (the top corners) of the breastplate, to fasten it to the ouches on the ephod's shoulder-pieces (vv.14, 25, the chains, the gold cords joining the breastplate to the ephod, the wreathen work, twisted cords of gold, the joining, the breastplate on the heart joined to the shoulders); the wreathen gold chains at the ends of the breastplate (joining it to the ephod) — the joining (the gold cords); 'of pure gold' (the chains of pure gold, the joining, the breastplate joined to the shoulders by gold chains, vv.25, the providence, the breastplate on the heart joined to the stones on the shoulders, the people borne on heart and shoulders, in love and strength) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make upon the breastplate chains at the ends of wreathen work of pure gold
the wreathen chains of the breastplate
the gold cords joining the breastplate to the shoulders (vv.14, 25)
the people borne on heart and shoulders, in love and strength

schools.summary: The gold rings of the breastplate: 'and thou shalt make upon the breastplate two rings of gold, and shalt put the two rings on the two ends of the breastplate' — two gold rings put on the two (upper) ends of the breastplate, to receive the wreathen chains (vv.22, 24, the rings, for the chains, joining the breastplate to the ephod, the placement, on the upper ends, near the shoulders, the joining, the breastplate fastened to the shoulders); the two gold rings (for the chains, on the ends of the breastplate) — the provision (the joining); 'the two ends of the breastplate' (the rings on the two upper ends, near the shoulders, for the chains, vv.24, the providence, the breastplate joined to the shoulders by the rings and chains, the people on heart and shoulders joined) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make upon the breastplate two rings of gold, and shalt put the two rings on the two ends of the breastplate
the gold rings of the breastplate
the rings for the chains, on the upper ends near the shoulders
the breastplate joined to the shoulders (vv.24)

schools.summary: The chains put in the rings: 'and thou shalt put the two wreathen chains of gold in the two rings which are on the ends of the breastplate' — the two wreathen gold chains put through the two rings on the ends of the breastplate, joining it to the ouches on the shoulders (vv.25, the joining, the breastplate fastened to the shoulders by the chains, the provision, the breastplate held up by the chains to the shoulder-pieces); the chains through the rings (joining the breastplate to the ephod) — the joining (the provision); 'which are on the ends of the breastplate' (the rings on the ends, receiving the chains, the joining, the breastplate to the shoulders, vv.25, the providence, the breastplate on the heart joined by gold chains to the stones on the shoulders, the people borne on heart and shoulders) — Gill.

themes: And thou shalt put the two wreathen chains of gold in the two rings which are on the ends of the breastplate
the chains put in the rings
the breastplate joined to the shoulders by the chains (vv.25)
the people borne on heart and shoulders

schools.summary: The chains fastened to the shoulderpieces: 'and the other two ends of the two wreathen chains thou shalt fasten in the two ouches, and put them on the shoulderpieces of the ephod before it' — the other ends of the chains fastened to the two ouches (the gold settings of the onyx stones, vv.13) on the shoulder-pieces of the ephod, in front (the joining, the breastplate joined by the chains to the shoulder-stones, the provision, the breastplate held up to the shoulders, the heart joined to the shoulders, the placement, the breastplate in front, on the breast); the chains fastened to the ouches on the shoulders (joining the breastplate to the shoulder-stones) — the joining (the provision); 'put them on the shoulderpieces of the ephod before it' (the breastplate joined to the shoulder-pieces in front, the providence, the breastplate on the heart joined to the stones on the shoulders, the people borne on heart and shoulders, in love and strength, the names on the heart and the names on the shoulders joined) — Gill.

themes: And the other two ends of the two wreathen chains thou shalt fasten in the two ouches, and put them on the shoulderpieces of the ephod before it
the chains fastened to the shoulderpieces
the breastplate joined to the shoulder-stones (vv.13)
the names on the heart and on the shoulders joined, the people borne in love and strength

schools.summary: The lower rings of the breastplate: 'and thou shalt make two rings of gold, and thou shalt put them upon the two ends of the breastplate in the border thereof, which is in the side of the ephod inward' — two more gold rings put on the two lower ends of the breastplate, on the inner side (toward the ephod), to fasten the lower edge of the breastplate to the ephod (vv.27-28, the lower rings, for binding the breastplate at the bottom, the provision, the breastplate held firm, top and bottom, against the ephod); the two lower rings (on the inner side of the breastplate) — the provision (the binding); 'which is in the side of the ephod inward' (the rings on the inner side, toward the ephod, hidden, the binding, the lower breastplate fastened to the ephod, vv.27-28, the providence, the breastplate held firm against the ephod, the heart fixed, not loosed) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make two rings of gold, and thou shalt put them upon the two ends of the breastplate in the border thereof, which is in the side of the ephod inward
the lower rings of the breastplate
the rings for binding the lower breastplate to the ephod (vv.27-28)
the breastplate held firm, the heart fixed

schools.summary: The lower rings of the ephod: 'and two other rings of gold thou shalt make, and shalt put them on the two sides of the ephod underneath, toward the forepart thereof, over against the other coupling thereof, above the curious girdle of the ephod' — two more gold rings put on the ephod, low down on the front, above the girdle, opposite the lower rings of the breastplate (vv.26, the rings, for binding the breastplate to the ephod at the bottom, the placement, low, above the girdle, matching the breastplate's lower rings, the provision, the breastplate bound firm to the ephod); the two lower rings of the ephod (low, on the front, above the girdle) — the provision (the binding); 'above the curious girdle of the ephod' (the rings above the girdle, low on the ephod, the binding, the breastplate bound to the ephod by these rings and the breastplate's lower rings, vv.28, the providence, the breastplate held firm, not loosed from the ephod) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And two other rings of gold thou shalt make, and shalt put them on the two sides of the ephod underneath, toward the forepart thereof... above the curious girdle of the ephod
the lower rings of the ephod
the rings for binding the breastplate to the ephod at the bottom (vv.26, 28)
the breastplate held firm, not loosed from the ephod

schools.summary: The breastplate bound to the ephod that it be not loosed: 'and they shall bind the breastplate by the rings thereof unto the rings of the ephod with a lace of blue, that it may be above the curious girdle of the ephod, and that the breastplate be not loosed from the ephod' — the lower rings of the breastplate bound to the lower rings of the ephod with a blue lace (cord), so that the breastplate sat firm above the girdle and was not loosed from the ephod (the binding, the breastplate fixed firmly to the ephod, top by chains, bottom by the blue lace, the firmness, the breastplate not to be loosed, the heart held fast to the shoulders); the breastplate not loosed (held fast to the ephod) — the figure (the people on the heart and the shoulders held fast together, never loosed, the high priest never parted from the people he bears, a figure of Christ's unfailing bearing of his people, never loosing them from his heart and his strength, the security of the people in the priest, John 10:28-29, 'neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand,' the inseparable union, the people bound to the priest, never loosed); the breastplate bound fast (the heart and shoulders joined, the people held secure) — the providence (the people borne on heart and shoulders, never loosed, the figure of Christ's sure and unfailing bearing of his own) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And they shall bind the breastplate by the rings thereof unto the rings of the ephod with a lace of blue... that the breastplate be not loosed from the ephod
the breastplate bound to the ephod that it be not loosed
the people on heart and shoulders held fast, never loosed
a figure of Christ's unfailing bearing of his people, the security of the people in the priest (John 10:28-29)

schools.summary: The high priest bearing the tribes on his heart: 'and Aaron shall bear the names of the children of Israel in the breastplate of judgment upon his heart, when he goeth in unto the holy place, for a memorial before the LORD continually' — the figure — 'Aaron shall bear the names of the children of Israel in the breastplate of judgment upon his heart, when he goeth in unto the holy place': the high priest bearing the names of the tribes (the twelve stones) on the breastplate over his heart, whenever he entered the holy place, for a memorial before the LORD continually (the witnesses noting the lesson — the high priest bearing the people on his heart, the place of love and affection, as on his shoulders, the place of strength, vv.12, the people borne in love and in strength, presented before God, remembered continually, a figure of Christ the great High Priest bearing his people on his heart, loving them, presenting them before God, the names on the heart, the people loved and remembered, Christ bearing his church on his heart, in his love, ever appearing for them, Hebrews 7:25; 9:24, the people graven on his heart as on his hands, Song of Songs 8:6, 'set me as a seal upon thine heart,' the love of the priest for the people, borne continually before God); 'for a memorial before the LORD continually' (the people remembered continually, borne on the heart, presented before God always, the providence, the high priest bearing the people on his heart continually, the figure of Christ ever bearing and presenting his people in love, Hebrews 7:25, 'he ever liveth to make intercession for them') — Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the high priest bearing the names on his heart signify?

sides.position: The high priest bearing the people on his heart, the place of love, as on his shoulders the place of strength, presenting them before God continually - a figure of Christ bearing his people on his heart in love, ever appearing and interceding for them (Hebrews 7:25; 9:24; Song of Songs 8:6)

themes: And Aaron shall bear the names of the children of Israel in the breastplate of judgment upon his heart, when he goeth in unto the holy place, for a memorial before the LORD continually
the high priest bearing the tribes on his heart (the crux of bearing the people in love)
the people borne on the heart, the place of love, as on the shoulders the place of strength
a figure of Christ bearing his people on his heart, ever interceding for them (Hebrews 7:25; 9:24; Song of Songs 8:6)

schools.summary: The Urim and Thummim, the means of God's judgment: 'and thou shalt put in the breastplate of judgment the Urim and the Thummim; and they shall be upon Aaron's heart, when he goeth in before the LORD: and Aaron shall bear the judgment of the children of Israel upon his heart before the LORD continually' — the Urim and Thummim (Hebrew, 'lights and perfections') put in (or were) the breastplate of judgment, on Aaron's heart, by which God's judgment (his will, his answer) was sought and given (the Urim and Thummim, the mysterious means of divine revelation, by which the high priest enquired of God for the people, Numbers 27:21; 1 Samuel 28:6, the oracle of God); the names — 'Urim and Thummim' ('lights and perfections'); the witnesses weighing what they were (variously held: the twelve stones themselves, or distinct objects in the breastplate-pouch, by which God gave answers, perhaps by the shining of the stones, or by an audible voice, the manner uncertain, the use, the means of seeking God's judgment, his will, for the people, in hard cases, Numbers 27:21, the figure — the Urim and Thummim a figure of Christ, in whom are the true lights and perfections, the perfect revelation of God's will, the oracle of God, Christ the light, John 8:12, and the perfection, in whom God's will is fully revealed, Hebrews 1:1-2; Colossians 2:3, 'in whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge,' Christ our oracle, in whom we know God's mind); the high priest bearing the 'judgment' (the means of God's judgment, the Urim and Thummim) on his heart — the people's cause and God's answer borne by the priest (the providence, the high priest the medium of God's judgment for the people, a figure of Christ through whom we know and obtain God's will) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What were the Urim and Thummim and what do they signify?

sides.position: The mysterious means of divine revelation ('lights and perfections') by which the high priest sought God's judgment for the people (Numbers 27:21; 1 Samuel 28:6) - the exact form uncertain; a figure of Christ the true Lights and Perfections, the oracle of God in whom his will is fully revealed (John 8:12; Hebrews 1:1-2; Colossians 2:3)

themes: And thou shalt put in the breastplate of judgment the Urim and the Thummim... and Aaron shall bear the judgment of the children of Israel upon his heart before the LORD continually
the Urim and Thummim, the means of God's judgment
the means of divine revelation, seeking God's will for the people (Numbers 27:21)
a figure of Christ the true Lights and Perfections, the oracle of God (John 8:12; Hebrews 1:1-2; Colossians 2:3)

schools.summary: The robe of the ephod all of blue: 'and thou shalt make the robe of the ephod all of blue' — the robe (worn under the ephod, over the coat) made all of blue (the robe of the ephod, the long blue robe, the second of the high priest's garments, vv.31-35, with bells and pomegranates on its hem, the blue, the heavenly color, all blue, the figure, some note, of the heavenly character of Christ's priesthood, the heavenly robe); the robe (worn under the ephod, the long blue robe) — the figure (the heavenly robe, the blue of heaven, the heavenly priesthood, or the righteousness of Christ); 'all of blue' (the robe wholly blue, the heavenly color, unmixed, the figure, the heavenly character, the blue of heaven, the robe of the heavenly High Priest, the providence, the blue robe, the figure of the heavenly priesthood, with the bells and pomegranates of testimony and fruit, vv.33-35) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And thou shalt make the robe of the ephod all of blue
the robe of the ephod all of blue
the heavenly color, the figure of the heavenly priesthood
with the bells and pomegranates of testimony and fruit (vv.33-35)

schools.summary: The reinforced opening of the robe: 'and there shall be an hole in the top of it, in the midst thereof: it shall have a binding of woven work round about the hole of it, as it were the hole of an habergeon, that it be not rent' — an opening in the top (for the head), bound with woven work (a strong, reinforced border, like the neck of a coat of mail, a habergeon), that it not be torn (the opening, for the head, the robe slipped over the head, the binding, the reinforced border, that the robe not rend, the strength, the garment not to be torn); the head-hole bound with woven work (reinforced, like a coat of mail's neck) — the strength (the reinforcement); 'that it be not rent' (the robe not to be torn, the strong border, the seamless strength, the figure, some note, the unrent robe, the figure of the unbroken, seamless priesthood, or of Christ's seamless robe, John 19:23-24, not rent, the integrity of the priestly garment, the providence, the robe whole, not rent, the figure of the perfect, unbroken priesthood of Christ) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And there shall be an hole in the top of it... it shall have a binding of woven work round about the hole of it, as it were the hole of an habergeon, that it be not rent
the reinforced opening of the robe
the strong border, that the robe not be torn
a figure of the whole, unbroken priesthood of Christ (John 19:23-24)

schools.summary: The pomegranates and bells on the hem of the robe: 'and beneath upon the hem of it thou shalt make pomegranates of blue, and of purple, and of scarlet, round about the hem thereof; and bells of gold between them round about' — on the hem of the blue robe pomegranates (of blue, purple, scarlet yarn) alternating with golden bells, all around (the pomegranates, the fruit, the figure of fruitfulness, the fruit of the priesthood, or the fruit of the Spirit, Galatians 5:22-23, the bells, the golden bells, sounding, the figure of testimony, the sound of the priest's ministry); the pomegranates (the fruit on the hem) — the figure (the fruit of the priesthood, the fruitfulness of grace, the fruit of the Spirit, the priest adorned with fruit); 'bells of gold between them round about' (the golden bells, alternating with the pomegranates, sounding as the priest moved, vv.35, the figure, the bells the sound of testimony, the word, the sound of the priest's ministry heard, the fruit and the sound together, the doctrine and the life, the word and the fruit, the priest's ministry both heard and fruitful, 1 Corinthians 13:1, the sound not empty but with fruit, the providence, the bells and pomegranates, the testimony and the fruit of the priesthood, a figure of the ministry of the word with fruit) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And beneath upon the hem of it thou shalt make pomegranates of blue, and of purple, and of scarlet, round about the hem thereof; and bells of gold between them round about
the pomegranates and bells on the hem of the robe
the pomegranates the fruit (the fruit of the Spirit, Galatians 5:22-23), the bells the sound of testimony
the word and the fruit together, the ministry heard and fruitful

schools.summary: The alternating bell and pomegranate: 'a golden bell and a pomegranate, a golden bell and a pomegranate, upon the hem of the robe round about' — the golden bells and the pomegranates alternating all around the hem (bell, pomegranate, bell, pomegranate, the alternation, the sound and the fruit together, in unbroken succession, the figure, the bells of testimony and the pomegranates of fruit, ever together, the word and the fruit, the ministry both heard and fruitful); the bell and pomegranate alternating (the sound and the fruit, together) — the figure (the testimony and the fruit ever joined, the word with fruit, the doctrine and the life); 'upon the hem of the robe round about' (the bells and pomegranates all around the hem, the unbroken succession, the providence, the sound and the fruit ever together, the figure of the ministry of the word joined with fruitfulness, the bells alone empty, the fruit alone silent, but together the full ministry) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: A golden bell and a pomegranate, a golden bell and a pomegranate, upon the hem of the robe round about
the alternating bell and pomegranate
the sound and the fruit ever together, the word with fruit
the full ministry of the priesthood, heard and fruitful

schools.summary: The sound of the bells heard as the priest ministers: 'and it shall be upon Aaron to minister: and his sound shall be heard when he goeth in unto the holy place before the LORD, and when he cometh out, that he die not' — the robe with its bells worn by Aaron to minister, that the sound of the bells be heard as he went in and out of the holy place, on pain of death if he came without it (the sound, the bells heard, the priest's ministry announced, the requirement, the priest to wear the robe, the sound to be heard, that he die not); the sound heard (the bells announcing the priest's coming before God) — the figure (the sound of the priest's ministry, the word heard, the testimony, the priest not coming before God silently, but with the sound of his ministry, perhaps the figure of Christ's intercession, ever heard before God, or the gospel sound); 'that he die not' (the priest to wear the robe and bells, on pain of death, the holy requirement, the priest not to approach God without the appointed garments and the sound, the providence, the priest's ministry heard, the appointed approach to God, the warning, the priest to come as God appointed, or die) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And it shall be upon Aaron to minister: and his sound shall be heard when he goeth in unto the holy place before the LORD, and when he cometh out, that he die not
the sound of the bells heard as the priest ministers
the priest's ministry announced and heard before God
the appointed approach to God, on pain of death if he came without it

schools.summary: The golden plate engraved HOLINESS TO THE LORD: 'and thou shalt make a plate of pure gold, and grave upon it, like the engravings of a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD' — the golden plate — 'a plate of pure gold... HOLINESS TO THE LORD': a plate (a shining gold plate, a diadem) made for the front of the high priest's mitre, engraved like a signet with the words 'HOLINESS TO THE LORD' (or 'Holy to Jehovah,' the witnesses noting the meaning — the high priest's crown bearing the inscription of holiness, marking the holiness required of the priest, the consecration to God, the dedication of the whole priesthood and people to the LORD, the lesson — holiness the great requirement and mark of God's service, the priest holy, the people holy, 'be ye holy; for I am holy,' Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16, the figure of Christ, the truly Holy One, the high priest's plate a figure of Christ's perfect holiness, 'holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners,' Hebrews 7:26, the Holy One of God, who alone is perfectly 'holiness to the LORD,' and in whom his people are made holy, and the saints, a holy priesthood, 'holiness to the LORD' written on them, Zechariah 14:20-21); the inscription engraved like a signet (deeply, permanently, on the gold plate) — the permanence (the mark of holiness graven, abiding, the dignity of the inscription); HOLINESS TO THE LORD — the consecration of the priesthood to God (the providence, the high priest crowned with holiness, the figure of Christ the Holy One, bearing the holiness in which his people are accepted, vv.38) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'HOLINESS TO THE LORD' on the high priest's crown signify?

sides.position: The holiness required of God's service and the consecration of the priesthood to the LORD (Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16), a figure of Christ the truly Holy One, 'holy, harmless, undefiled' (Hebrews 7:26), in whom his people are made holy and accepted (vv.38; Zechariah 14:20-21)

themes: And thou shalt make a plate of pure gold, and grave upon it, like the engravings of a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD
the golden plate engraved HOLINESS TO THE LORD (the crux of holiness)
the holiness required of God's service, the consecration of the priesthood (Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16)
a figure of Christ the truly Holy One, in whom his people are made holy (Hebrews 7:26; Zechariah 14:20-21)

schools.summary: The plate set on the front of the mitre: 'and thou shalt put it on a blue lace, that it may be upon the mitre; upon the forefront of the mitre it shall be' — the golden plate fastened with a blue lace (cord) to the mitre (the turban), on the forefront (the forehead, the placement, the plate on the front of the mitre, on the high priest's forehead, conspicuous, the mitre, the turban, the head-dress of the high priest, the visible holiness, the inscription on the forehead, conspicuous, the mark of holiness borne openly); the plate fastened with a blue lace to the mitre — the placement (the binding); 'upon the forefront of the mitre it shall be' (the plate on the forehead, conspicuous, the mark of holiness openly borne, the figure, the holiness borne on the forehead, openly, as the saints sealed in the forehead, Revelation 7:3; 14:1, the open profession of holiness, the providence, the holiness conspicuous on the high priest's brow, the figure of Christ's open holiness and the open dedication of his people) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt put it on a blue lace, that it may be upon the mitre; upon the forefront of the mitre it shall be
the plate set on the front of the mitre
the holiness borne openly on the forehead
a figure of the saints sealed in the forehead (Revelation 7:3; 14:1)

schools.summary: The high priest bearing the iniquity of the holy things: 'and it shall be upon Aaron's forehead, that Aaron may bear the iniquity of the holy things, which the children of Israel shall hallow in all their holy gifts; and it shall be always upon his forehead, that they may be accepted before the LORD' — the figure — 'that Aaron may bear the iniquity of the holy things, which the children of Israel shall hallow... that they may be accepted before the LORD': the high priest, wearing the plate of holiness, bearing 'the iniquity of the holy things' (the imperfection, the sin that cleaves even to the holy gifts and offerings of the people), that the people and their gifts might be accepted before the LORD (the witnesses noting the lesson — even the holy things of the people defiled with sin, the imperfection of our best works and worship, the sin mingled with our holiest offerings, Isaiah 64:6, 'all our righteousnesses are as filthy rags,' the high priest, by his holiness, the plate 'HOLINESS TO THE LORD,' bearing and covering this iniquity, that the gifts be accepted, the figure of Christ — Christ our High Priest bearing the iniquity even of our holy things, his perfect holiness covering the imperfection of our worship and works, that we and our offerings be accepted in him, the acceptance 'in the beloved,' Ephesians 1:6, our spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God 'by Jesus Christ,' 1 Peter 2:5, Christ bearing our sin, even the sin of our holiness, and presenting us holy and accepted, Hebrews 9:14; Colossians 1:22); 'that they may be accepted before the LORD' (the people and their gifts accepted, through the high priest's holiness bearing their iniquity, the acceptance not in themselves but in the priest, the figure, our acceptance in Christ, not in ourselves, Christ's holiness the ground of our acceptance) — Wesley, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does the high priest bearing 'the iniquity of the holy things' signify?

sides.position: Even the holy gifts of the people are defiled with sin (Isaiah 64:6), and the high priest's holiness bears and covers this iniquity, that they be accepted - a figure of Christ our High Priest bearing the iniquity even of our holiest things, his holiness the ground of our acceptance in him (Ephesians 1:6; 1 Peter 2:5; Hebrews 9:14)

themes: And it shall be upon Aaron's forehead, that Aaron may bear the iniquity of the holy things... that they may be accepted before the LORD
the high priest bearing the iniquity of the holy things
even our holy gifts defiled with sin, the priest's holiness covering the iniquity (Isaiah 64:6)
a figure of Christ bearing the imperfection of our worship, our acceptance in him (Ephesians 1:6; 1 Peter 2:5; Hebrews 9:14)

schools.summary: The coat, mitre, and girdle of fine linen: 'and thou shalt embroider the coat of fine linen, and thou shalt make the mitre of fine linen, and thou shalt make the girdle of needlework' — the coat (the tunic, worn next the body, embroidered, of fine white linen), the mitre (the turban, of fine linen, bearing the golden plate), and the girdle (the sash, of needlework, the colored embroidery, the garments, the coat, mitre, girdle, completing the high priest's vestments, the fine linen, the white, pure linen, the figure of righteousness, Revelation 19:8); the embroidered coat of fine linen (the tunic, the white garment) — the figure (the fine linen, the righteousness, the purity of the priest); 'the mitre of fine linen, and... the girdle of needlework' (the mitre, the linen turban, with the golden plate, and the girdle, the embroidered sash, the providence, the garments of glory and beauty completed, the high priest fully clothed, the figure of Christ's priestly adornment and righteousness) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt embroider the coat of fine linen, and thou shalt make the mitre of fine linen, and thou shalt make the girdle of needlework
the coat, mitre, and girdle of fine linen
the white linen garments completing the high priest's adornment
the fine linen the figure of righteousness (Revelation 19:8)

schools.summary: The garments for Aaron's sons: 'and for Aaron's sons thou shalt make coats, and thou shalt make for them girdles, and bonnets shalt thou make for them, for glory and for beauty' — garments made also for Aaron's sons (the common priests): coats (tunics), girdles (sashes), and bonnets (caps, the priestly garments for the sons, simpler than the high priest's, but also for glory and beauty, the common priesthood, the sons clothed for their ministry); the coats, girdles, and bonnets of the common priests (the simpler vestments) — the priesthood clothed (the figure, the priests, the figure of the believers, a royal priesthood, clothed for service); 'for glory and for beauty' (the garments of the sons also for glory and beauty, as the high priest's, vv.2, the dignity of the priesthood, the providence, the whole priesthood clothed for glory and beauty, the figure of the priestly people clothed in righteousness, Revelation 19:8, the dignity of God's service) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And for Aaron's sons thou shalt make coats, and thou shalt make for them girdles, and bonnets shalt thou make for them, for glory and for beauty
the garments for Aaron's sons
the common priests clothed for their ministry, for glory and beauty (vv.2)
the figure of the priestly people clothed in righteousness (Revelation 19:8)

schools.summary: The anointing and consecration of the priests: 'and thou shalt put them upon Aaron thy brother, and his sons with him; and shalt anoint them, and consecrate them, and sanctify them, that they may minister unto me in the priest's office' — the priests to be clothed in the garments, anointed (with the holy oil), consecrated (Hebrew 'fill the hand,' ordained), and sanctified (set apart) for the priesthood (the consecration, the investing, anointing, ordaining, setting apart of the priests, ch. 29, the threefold, anoint, consecrate, sanctify, the full induction into the office); the anointing (with the holy oil, the figure of the Spirit's anointing, 1 John 2:20, 27) — the consecration by oil (the figure, the priests anointed, the figure of Christ the Anointed, and of believers anointed with the Spirit); 'consecrate them, and sanctify them, that they may minister unto me in the priest's office' (the ordaining and sanctifying, the priests set apart for God's service, ch. 29, the providence, the priests consecrated, anointed, sanctified, the figure of Christ and his priestly people, consecrated to God's service) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And thou shalt put them upon Aaron thy brother, and his sons with him; and shalt anoint them, and consecrate them, and sanctify them, that they may minister unto me in the priest's office
the anointing and consecration of the priests
the priests clothed, anointed, ordained, and set apart (ch. 29)
the anointing a figure of the Spirit, of Christ the Anointed and his priestly people (1 John 2:20)

schools.summary: The linen breeches to cover the priests' nakedness: 'and thou shalt make them linen breeches to cover their nakedness; from the loins even unto the thighs they shall reach' — linen breeches (drawers, undergarments) made to cover the priests' nakedness, from the loins to the thighs (the breeches, the undermost garment, for modesty and decency in the service, the covering of nakedness, the shame of nakedness covered, the decency of God's service, the reverence in approaching God); the covering of nakedness (the shame of the flesh covered, the decency required, the modesty in God's service) — the figure (the nakedness of sin and shame covered, the need of covering to approach God, the figure of the righteousness that covers our shame, Revelation 3:18; Genesis 3:7, 21); 'from the loins even unto the thighs they shall reach' (the breeches reaching from loins to thighs, the decent covering, the reverence, the decency and modesty in God's service, the providence, the covering of nakedness, the figure of the need of righteousness to cover our shame before God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And thou shalt make them linen breeches to cover their nakedness; from the loins even unto the thighs they shall reach
the linen breeches to cover the priests' nakedness
the shame of nakedness covered, the decency of God's service
a figure of the righteousness that covers our shame before God (Genesis 3:21; Revelation 3:18)

schools.summary: The priests clothed to bear not iniquity and die: 'and they shall be upon Aaron, and upon his sons, when they come in unto the tabernacle of the congregation, or when they come near unto the altar to minister in the holy place; that they bear not iniquity, and die: it shall be a statute for ever unto him and his seed after him' — the priests to wear the breeches (and all the garments) whenever they entered the tabernacle or came near the altar to minister, that they bear not iniquity and die (the requirement, the priests to be properly clothed in God's service, on pain of death, the holiness, the decency and reverence required, the priest not to approach God carelessly or uncovered); the warning (the priest to be clothed, lest he bear iniquity and die) — the holiness of God's service (the careless or uncovered approach a deadly sin, Leviticus 10:1-2, the reverence required, the danger of approaching God amiss); 'it shall be a statute for ever unto him and his seed after him' (the perpetual statute, the requirement for all generations of priests, the providence, the priest to come clothed and covered, the reverence in God's service, the figure of the need of the covering of righteousness to approach God, the statute for ever pointing to the abiding need of righteousness in worship, fulfilled in Christ, who clothes his people for acceptable service, Revelation 19:8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, Ellicott.

themes: And they shall be upon Aaron, and upon his sons, when they come in unto the tabernacle of the congregation, or when they come near unto the altar to minister in the holy place; that they bear not iniquity, and die: it shall be a statute for ever
the priests clothed to bear not iniquity and die
the reverence and covering required to approach God (Leviticus 10:1-2)
the figure of the righteousness that clothes us for acceptable service (Revelation 19:8)
